explanation:
We extract three main constraints from the input: 
1. The matrix at each DU must be updated with frequency f (i.e., globally, in every time unit, each update must occur)
2. Every DU can update its navigation data every second (i.e., for all DUs, updating happens at least every time unit)
3. Each DM must transmit its matrix to other DMs every time unit, and any DM can receive up to 49 matrices per time unit (i.e., in every time unit, at most 49 receptions per DM, and every pairwise transmission occurs every time unit).
We have atomic propositions for sending and receiving matrix updates between DMs: matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2, matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm2, etc. 
Let's express as LTL:
1. Periodic transmission: 'Globally, in every step, DM1 sends update to DM2, DM3, DM4, DM5': G(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2), etc. for all DMs.
2. Periodic reception: 'Globally, in every step, DM1 receives updates from DM2, DM3, DM4, DM5': G(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm2), etc. for all DMs.
3. Each DU can update navigation data every second: G(F(dm1_performs_update)), assuming dm1_performs_update maps to 'update'.
(If we wanted to bound the reception to 49 per time unit, we'd need counters, which are not directly expressible in pure LTL and not supported by the given atomic propositions – we will express just the periodic reception instead).
Thus, we conjoin all the above.

explanation_dict.natural_language: Every period (time unit), DM1 sends matrix updates to DM2, DM3, DM4, DM5
Every period (time unit), DM1 receives matrix updates from DM2, DM3, DM4, DM5
Every DM can update its navigation data every period

output_LTL: G(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2) & G(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm3) & G(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm4) & G(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm5) & G(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm2) & G(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm3) & G(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm4) & G(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm5) & G(F(dm1_performs_update))

explanation: We revise the LTL formula to use only permitted atomic propositions. The essence of the requirement is: (1) matrix updates must be sent from DM1 to all other DMs with a frequency of (at most) 1 time unit (representable by requiring that these send actions occur infinitely often), (2) each DM1 must receive matrix updates from the others (implied by the corresponding receive actions), and (3) DM1 performs an update every time unit. The requirement that no more than 49 are received per time unit cannot be natively expressed in LTL, so it is noted but not included in the LTL formula.

explanation_dict.natural_language: The matrix update is sent from DM1 to DM2, DM3, DM4, and DM5 with period 1 time unit
DM1 receives a matrix update from DM2, DM3, DM4, DM5 infinitely often
DM1 performs an update every time unit (every next state)
No more than 49 matrices received per time unit (not expressible in LTL, informally noted)

output_LTL:
(GF(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2) & GF(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm3) & GF(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm4) & GF(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm5))
& (GF(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm2) & GF(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm3) & GF(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm4) & GF(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm5))
& G X(dm1_performs_update)

explanation: The specification states that each local matrix M for a DU_i must be updated with a frequency f, such that any DU can update its navigation data every second. To ensure this, (1) each DM must transmit its local matrix to the other DMs every time unit, and (2) any DM must be able to receive and process up to 49 matrix updates every time unit. These aspects mix timing and throughput requirements. Since standard LTL does not model actual numbers or explicit timing, but allows us to express temporal/causality properties, we'll focus on the periodic sending (every time unit) and 'processing all received updates in every time unit.' We'll capture: If a clock event occurs, then in the next state, each DM must send its local matrix updates to the other DMs. Similarly, after a clock tick, if a matrix update is received, then in the next state, the DM must perform an update (to represent the processing). Since there are 4 updates per DM (1 to each other DM), and 49 as an upper bound, we capture that in each unit where the clock ticks, all processing transitions can occur, which in LTL we capture as an implication for all updates received within a clock tick interval. Approximate the requirements as: Globally, whenever a clock occurs, in the next state all matrix updates from DM1 to DM2, DM3, DM4, and DM5 are sent, and whenever a matrix update from DM2, DM3, DM4, or DM5 is received by DM1, the corresponding update by DM1 happens in the next state.

explanation_dict.natural_language: On every clock tick, DM1 sends matrix updates to DM2, DM3, DM4, and DM5 in the next moment.
On every clock tick, if DM1 receives a matrix update from one DM, it performs an update in the next moment.

output_LTL: G(clock_occurred -> X(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2 & matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm3 & matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm4 & matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm5)) & G((clock_occurred & (matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm2 | matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm3 | matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm4 | matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm5)) -> X(dm1_performs_update))

explanation:
This specification combines periodic updates and timing constraints. There are three main points: (1) Each DM must transmit its local matrix every 1 time unit, (2) Any DM can receive and elaborate up to 49 matrices in 1 time unit (implying that receiving more than 49 in one time unit should never happen), and (3) Each DU can update navigation data every second, which follows from the first property.

Let us formalize:
1. For each DM, in every time unit (e.g., triggered by a 'clock_occurred'), for every pair of DMs (for DM1, 'matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2', ... etc), the update must be sent.
2. In every time unit, no DM should receive more than 49 matrix updates, i.e., it is not possible to receive more than 49 separate updates in the same time unit.

Assuming atomic propositions for each matrix update send and receive operation, we formalize these requirements step by step.

explanation_dict.natural_language: Each DM must send its matrix update to other DMs every 1 time unit.
Any DM must not receive more than 49 matrix updates in 1 time unit.
Each DU can update navigation data every second (this is a consequence of the first property).

output_LTL: G(clock_occurred -> (matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2 & matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm3 & matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm4 & matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm5 & dui_sends_local_matrix_to_services & !(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm2 & matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm3 & matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm4 & matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm5)))

explanation:
The specification describes three constraints: (1) each DU updates its navigation data every second (i.e., every time unit, a DU's matrix should be updated by receiving new matrix updates from other DMs), (2) each DM must send its local matrix instance to every other DM every 1 time unit, (3) any DM is able to receive and elaborate up to 49 matrices in 1 time unit (can be interpreted as 'at most 49 updates per time unit', which is more a bound/resource property and usually not modeled directly in LTL). The first two are captured in LTL:

1. Each DM sends matrix updates to all others every time unit: This can be captured with GF (infinitely often / every time unit, if time is discrete). So, for example, for DM1 to DM2: GF(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2). This generalizes to all DMs: e.g., for each pair i != j, GF(matrix_update_sent_dmi_to_dmj).

2. Each DM receives matrix updates from all others every time unit: Similarly, for DM1: GF(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm2), etc., for all other DMs. In general, each DMi should 'eventually' (actually, every time unit) receive an update from each DMj (j ≠ i).

3. The 'elaborate up to 49 matrices' is a count property and not natively expressed in LTL, so is omitted.

As a sample, for DM1 and 4 others (DM2..DM5):
G(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2) && G(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm3) && G(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm4) && G(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm5)
(similarly for sends from each DM to each other).

For conciseness, using the properties supplied, the main temporal assertions are:
G(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2) && G(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm3) && G(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm4) && G(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm5) && G(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm2) && G(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm3) && G(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm4) && G(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm5)
(The same applies symmetrically for other DMs; we're illustrating for DM1.)

explanation_dict.natural_language: Each DM sends its local matrix instance to every other DM with a period of 1 time unit
Each DM is able to receive a matrix from every other DM every time unit

output_LTL: G(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2) && G(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm3) && G(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm4) && G(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm5) && G(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm2) && G(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm3) && G(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm4) && G(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm5)

explanation:
The sentence is complex and refers to a periodic update (every second) of a matrix M in all DUs, implying all DMs must send their local matrix to all other DMs every unit time, and each DM must be able to receive and process up to 49 matrices per unit time. We decompose this into two core LTL requirements:

1. Every DM must send its matrix update to every other DM within every period (1 time unit). This means that, globally, at every clock tick, the matrix update must be sent from each DM to all others.
2. Every DM must be able to receive and elaborate up to 49 matrices per unit time. This can be interpreted as, at every clock tick, the DM may receive up to 49 distinct matrix updates, but at least, for safety, we express that 'if a DM receives up to 49 matrices, it can elaborate them'. Since LTL cannot count, we phrase this as: Globally, if a matrix update is received from any DM, then an update operation is performed by DM1 (as an example DM).

We use atomic propositions for sending matrix updates between DMs and for processing updates. For brevity and generality, the translation focuses primarily on DM1, as the structure can be generalized to all DMs by similar formulas.

explanation_dict.natural_language: The matrix update is sent from DM1 to DM2, DM3, DM4, DM5 every period (1 time unit)
explanation_dict.LTL: G(clock_occurred -> (F matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2 & F matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm3 & F matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm4 & F matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm5))

explanation_dict.natural_language: If DM1 receives a matrix update from DM2/3/4/5, then DM1 performs an update after receiving it
explanation_dict.LTL: G((matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm2 | matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm3 | matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm4 | matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm5) -> F dm1_performs_update)

output_LTL: G(clock_occurred -> (F matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2 & F matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm3 & F matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm4 & F matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm5)) & G((matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm2 | matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm3 | matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm4 | matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm5) -> F dm1_performs_update)

explanation: We translated the natural language requirements about matrix updates using only the provided atomic propositions. We enforced that the DM1 instance is updated infinitely often, that DM1 sends updates to each of the other DMs globally, that those updates are received globally, and that whenever DM1 sends an update to another DM, DM1 eventually receives a corresponding matrix update (assuming bidirectional updates, represented by available propositions). Counting up to 49 cannot be directly encoded in LTL, so we note that constraint is omitted.

explanation_dict.natural_language: The local instance of matrix M for DU_i (represented as dm1_performs_update) must be updated infinitely often.
DM1 sends a matrix update to DM2, DM3, DM4, and DM5 globally.
DM1 receives a matrix update from DM2, DM3, DM4, and DM5 globally.
Whenever DM1 sends a matrix update to DM2, eventually DM1 receives a matrix update from DM2; and similarly for DM3, DM4, and DM5.
explanation_dict.LTL: G(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2 -> F matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm2) & G(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm3 -> F matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm3) & G(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm4 -> F matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm4) & G(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm5 -> F matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm5)

output_LTL: GF(dm1_performs_update) & G(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2) & G(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm3) & G(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm4) & G(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm5) & G(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm2) & G(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm3) & G(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm4) & G(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm5) & G(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2 -> F matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm2) & G(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm3 -> F matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm3) & G(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm4 -> F matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm4) & G(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm5 -> F matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm5)

explanation:
To translate the requirement:
- The main point is that each local matrix M in a DU should be updated at a frequency f, so every DU can update its navigation every second. Thus, every DU updates once every second.
- To satisfy this, each DM must transmit its local matrix to other DMs every time unit (every second).
- Additionally, any DM can receive and process up to 49 matrices in one time unit, i.e., it can, in principle, receive one from each DM in that window.

From the given atomic propositions, we'll use:
- "matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2", "matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm3", ... : represents DM1 sending to DM2, etc. (but the idea is generic for any DM).
- "dm1_performs_update": perform the local update in DM1 (again, generically, for any DM).

Let's break it into 3 subformulae:
1. 'EVERY DM transmits its local matrix to the OTHER DMs with period 1 time unit' means, for instance, for DM1 to DM2: Globally, at every moment, within the next time unit, the event 'matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2' occurs, and similarly for all DM pairs.
2. 'Any DM receives and processes up to 49 matrices in one time unit' means that at no time are more than 49 matrices received in any interval of length 1. (Standard LTL cannot express quantitative bounds >1; with LTL, we can only say things like 'at most once per time unit', not 'at most 49 times per unit'. We will note this limitation.)
3. 'Each DU can update its navigation data every second' means Globally, every second, every DU performs the update, i.e., G F[0,1] dm1_performs_update, but in LTL, as G F dm1_performs_update.

Therefore, the LTL formulas for a concrete DM (say, DM1) and its interactions are:
1. G F (matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2) (and similarly for the other DMs) - meaning: "DM1 sends to DM2 infinitely often" (actually, we want 'every time unit', but in pure LTL, we can only enforce 'infinitely often').
2. G F (dm1_performs_update) - "DM1 performs update infinitely often" ('every second' in LTL can only be 'infinitely often' unless we have clocks).

For the reception limitation (49 matrices per time unit), we note that this cannot be encoded in plain LTL.

Thus, combining for all DMs (say, N=5 DMs), the LTL specification is the conjunction over all DM pairs, and for each DM, the update. For illustration, we show it for DM1:
G F (matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2) & G F (matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm3) & G F (matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm4) & G F (matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm5) & G F (dm1_performs_update)

Full formula:
G F (matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2) & G F (matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm3) & G F (matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm4) & G F (matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm5) & G F (dm1_performs_update)

This encodes that all intended communication and updates happen infinitely often, which, in the context of a clock or timed model, would correspond to "at every time unit."

explanation_dict.natural_language: Each DM must transmit its local matrix instance to the other DMs with a period of 1 time unit.
explanation_dict.LTL: G F (matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2) & G F (matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm3) & G F (matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm4) & G F (matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm5)

explanation_dict.natural_language: Any DM is able to receive and elaborate up to 49 matrices in 1 time unit.
explanation_dict.LTL: Cannot be directly encoded in pure LTL (requires counting or bounded operators).

explanation_dict.natural_language: Each DU can update its navigation data every second.

output_LTL: G F (matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2) & G F (matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm3) & G F (matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm4) & G F (matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm5) & G F (dm1_performs_update)

explanation:
The main requirement states that each DM transmits its local matrix to other DMs every 1 time unit, and any DM can process (receive and elaborate) up to 49 matrices per 1 time unit. We'll express the required parts that can be mapped given the atomic propositions provided.

1) Each DM must send its matrix update to other DMs every time unit. We'll use the example of DM1 sending to DM2-5;
2) Any DM must be able to receive up to 49 matrices per time unit: As atomic propositions exist for 'matrix update received by DM1 from DM2..5', we express that after a clock tick, it's possible to receive up to 49 matrices. With only 4 possible incoming atomic propositions, we can say at most that all possible incoming messages are eventually received in the next time step. This doesn't scale to 49 messages, but is the best representation given the provided set.

Therefore, we split the translation in two parts: (1) periodic transmission, (2) ability to receive all available matrices (4 in this example) in every time unit.

explanation_dict.natural_language: Each DM transmits its local matrix to other DMs with a period of 1 time unit.
Any DM can receive all available matrix updates from the others within every time unit.
explanation_dict.LTL: G (clock_occurred -> (F X matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm2 & F X matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm3 & F X matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm4 & F X matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm5))

output_LTL: G (clock_occurred -> (matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2 & matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm3 & matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm4 & matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm5)) & G (clock_occurred -> (F X matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm2 & F X matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm3 & F X matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm4 & F X matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm5))

explanation:
The requirement describes a distributed update protocol for a matrix M among DM (Distributed Module) components and DUs, implying:
1. Each DM must send its local matrix (M) to the other DMs every time unit (period 1).
2. Each DM is capable of receiving and processing up to 49 matrices per time unit.
3. Any DU can update its navigation data every second (meaning: each DU_i gets the needed update from some DM at least every time unit).

Let’s formalize these components using the best approximations with the provided atomic propositions.

--- Stepwise Breakdown ---

*1. Each DM transmits its local matrix to other DMs with period 1.*
This means for each pair of DMs (e.g., DM1 to DM2, DM3, DM4, DM5) a matrix update is sent every time unit. In LTL, we use the globally finally (GF) operator to indicate 'periodic' or 'infinitely often'.
Examples: GF(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2), etc. Repeating per sender/receiver combination.

*2. Any DM is able to receive and elaborate up to 49 matrices per unit time.*
This is a quantitative resource bound, difficult to encode strictly in pure LTL with only atomic propositions and standard operators, but we can interpret that 'each possible matrix update from other DMs can be received by a DM every time unit'.
So, for each DM1, periodically it receives the matrix updates from each DM2-5, within each time unit. Again, GF(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm2), etc., ensures this.

*3. A DU can update its navigation data every second.*
Assume this happens when it receives the matrix from a DM via the atomic event dui_sends_local_matrix_to_services.
So: GF(dui_sends_local_matrix_to_services).

-- Full Specification --
Therefore, we write the periodic sending and receiving for one sample DM (DM1), with the understanding the same pattern replicates for all DMs. (If needed for all DMs, we generalize accordingly.)

The formula could be:
GF(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2) & GF(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm3) & GF(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm4) & GF(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm5)
& GF(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm2) & GF(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm3) & GF(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm4) & GF(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm5)
& GF(dui_sends_local_matrix_to_services)

This means: each matrix is sent, received, and delivered to services infinitely often (i.e., at least once per time unit, in the ideal LTL abstraction).

-- Limitation Note --
The LTL formalism here abstracts away the exact numeric bound (49), focusing instead on per-pair liveness. To represent exact counts or restrict to 49 per time unit would require more expressive logic (e.g., counting extensions, not basic LTL).

explanation_dict.natural_language: Each DM transmits its local matrix instance to the other DMs with a period of 1 time unit.
Any DM is able to receive and elaborate up to 49 matrices in 1 time unit.
Each DU can update its navigation data every second.

output_LTL: (GF(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2) & GF(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm3) & GF(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm4) & GF(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm5)) & (GF(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm2) & GF(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm3) & GF(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm4) & GF(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm5)) & GF(dui_sends_local_matrix_to_services)
